votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specifications, checking for ascending order and ensuring no element appears more than twice.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specifications, including checking for ascending order and ensuring no element appears more than twice.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec for concrete inputs. count_occurrences counts elements correctly. no_more_than_two_duplicates checks all elements have count <= 2. is_ascending checks adjacent pairs which is equivalent to the Coq definition for sorted lists. is_sorted_spec returns True iff result matches the conjunction of both conditions. The Python implementations produce the same concrete results as the Coq definitions.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec for concrete inputs. count_occurrences counts elements correctly. no_more_than_two_duplicates checks all elements have count <= 2. is_ascending checks consecutive pairs which is equivalent to the Coq definition for sorted lists. is_sorted_spec returns whether result matches the conjunction of both conditions. The Python implementations will produce the same concrete judgments as the Coq definitions.

Python mirrors Coq: same occurrence counting and <=2 check, non-decreasing order via adjacent comparisons (equivalent by transitivity), and the spec checks result matches (is_ascending ∧ no_more_than_two_duplicates).
Counting occurrences, the at-most-two constraint, and nondecreasing order all match Coq’s semantics; is_sorted_spec checks result equals the conjunction, which aligns when result is a boolean.

Python matches Coq on counting occurrences, the duplicates constraint (elements occur at most twice), and non-decreasing order via adjacent comparisons, which is equivalent to the Coq is_ascending. The is_sorted_spec check returns true exactly when result is true iff both conditions hold.
